--- SOURCE 1: Track 16 Key Healthcare Quality Metrics for Better Outcomes ---
URL: https://www.clearpointstrategy.com/blog/quality-measures-in-healthcare

SUMMARY:
Revolutionize your healthcare quality assessment with these 16 metrics. Learn how to track and improve patient care, safety, and satisfaction.



--------------------------------------------------------------------------------


--- SOURCE 2: Metrics for Assessing Improvements in Primary Health Care - PMC ---
URL: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/

SUMMARY:
Performance metrics assess the complex interactions among primary care tenets of accessibility, a whole-person focus, integration and coordination of care, and ongoing relationships with individuals, families, and communities; primary health care principles of inclusion and equity, a focus on people’s needs, multilevel integration of health, collaborative policy dialogue, and stakeholder participation; basic and goal-directed health care, prioritization, development, and multilevel health outcomes. **Keywords:** primary care, primary health care, measurement, metrics, quality improvement, family, community, population health Public Health 92:1748–55 [DOI] [PMC free article] [PubMed] [Google Scholar] Health Care 20:37–48 [DOI] [PMC free article] [PubMed] [Google Scholar] Health Care 14:267–68 [DOI] [PubMed] [Google Scholar] Web Exclus W5–97–W5–107 [DOI] [PubMed] [Google Scholar%20Suppl.%20Web%20Exclus&title=The%20effects%20of%20specialist%20supply%20on%20populations%E2%80%99%20health:%20assessing%20the%20evidence&author=B%20Starfield&author=L%20Shi&author=A%20Grover&author=J%20Macinko&publication_year=2005&pages=W5%E2%80%9397-W5%E2%80%93107&pmid=15769797&doi=10.1377/hlthaff.w5.97&)] Public Health 102:S336–41 [DOI] [PMC free article] [PubMed] [Google Scholar] WHO, Geneva: http://www.who.int/topics/primary_health_care/en/ [Google Scholar]

[PMC Copyright notice](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/about/copyright/)

PMCID: PMC6360939 NIHMSID: NIHMS746739 PMID: [24641561](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/24641561/)

The publisher's version of this article is available at [Annu Rev Public Health](https://doi.org/10.1146/annurev-publhealth-032013-182438)

Abstract
--------

Metrics focus attention on what is important. Balanced metrics of primary health care inform purpose and aspiration as well as performance. Purpose in primary health care is about improving the health of people and populations in their community contexts. It is informed by metrics that include long-term, meaning- and relationship-focused perspectives. Aspirational uses of metrics inspire evolving insights and iterative improvement, using a collaborative, developmental perspective. Performance metrics assess the complex interactions among primary care tenets of accessibility, a whole-person focus, integration and coordination of care, and ongoing relationships with individuals, families, and communities; primary health care principles of inclusion and equity, a focus on people’s needs, multilevel integration of health, collaborative policy dialogue, and stakeholder participation; basic and goal-directed health care, prioritization, development, and multilevel health outcomes. Environments that support reflection, development, and collaborative action are necessary for metrics to advance health and minimize unintended consequences.

**Keywords:** primary care, primary health care, measurement, metrics, quality improvement, family, community, population health

INTRODUCTION
------------

The purpose of metrics is to focus attention on what is important that may be overlooked amidst pressing day-to-day concerns. In translating knowledge into action, metrics offer guidance and direction. Metrics can prompt us to contemplate the meaning and impact of activities too easily routinized ([30](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R30)). They allow us to consider what is being done well ([111](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R111)), what might be better left undone ([48](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R48)), what needs to be changed ([121](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R121)) and what is essential and therefore needs to be remain unchanged ([139](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R139)).

Metrics in primary health care serve three uses: to assess performance (processes and outcomes), to inform reflection on purpose, and to foster aspirational conversations that lead to ongoing development. Linking performance metrics to payment may increase the quality of narrowly defined care in the short term ([19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R19)) but risks unintended consequences in the longer term ([9](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R9), [19](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R19), [87](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R87)). In environments that support reflection, development, and collaborative action, metrics can enable primary health care to heal and develop itself ([79](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R79)) so that it can deliver on its promise to heal and develop the health of individuals, families, and communities ([141](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R141)). Below we describe what is important about primary health care, relevant metrics and their effective use, and environments needed to help metrics do more good than harm.

WHAT IS IMPORTANT ABOUT PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
-------------------------------------------

[Table 1](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#T1) highlights the concepts of primary care, primary _health_ care, community-oriented primary care, and the outcomes of improving primary health care. Primary care involves the complex interaction of accessibility, comprehensive attention to the full scope of an individual’s needs, and integration and coordination of care across multiple needs and settings, all within the context of sustained and ongoing relationships with individuals, families, and communities ([35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R35), [91](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R91), [133](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R133), [137](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R137)).

### Table 1.

What’s important about primary care

| Tenets of primary care ([35](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R35), [137](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R137)) |
| :--- |
| * * * |
| **The value of primary care emerges from synergy among:** |
| ∎ Accessibility as the first contact with the health care system |
| ∎ Accountability for addressing a large portion of personal health care needs (comprehensiveness) |
| ∎ Coordination of care across settings and integration of care of acute and (often comorbid) chronic illnesses, mental health, and prevention, guiding access to more narrowly focused care when needed |
| ∎ Sustained partnership and personal relationships over time with patients known in the contexts of family and community |
| * * * |
| **Primary health care** |
| According to the World Health Organization ([161](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R161)), “the ultimate goal of primary health care is better health for all. WHO has identified five key elements to achieving that goal: |
| ∎ reducing exclusion and social disparities in health (universal coverage reforms); |
| ∎ organizing health services around people’s needs and expectations (service delivery reforms); |
| ∎ integrating health into all sectors (public policy reforms); |
| ∎ pursuing collaborative models of policy dialogue (leadership reforms); and |
| ∎ increasing stakeholder participation.” |
| * * * |
| **Principles of community-oriented primary care** ([28](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R28), [109](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R109)) |
| ∎ Primary care tenets |
| ∎ Taking responsibility for a defined geographical or social community |
| ∎ A process that includes |
| - defining and characterizing the community |
| - conducting a community diagnosis |
| - developing and implementing an intervention |
| - monitoring the impact of the intervention |
| - involving the community to carry out the preceding four steps |
| * * * |
| **Outcomes of primary health care** ([4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R4), [138](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R138), [141](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R141)) |
| ∎ Better population health |
| ∎ Less health imbalance and inequity |
| ∎ Better health care quality |
| ∎ Less health care expenditure |

[Open in a new tab](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/table/T1/)

The concept of primary _health_ care, espoused by the World Health Organization ([161](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R161)) and others, positions primary care within larger social and political principles, including equity, population needs, multisectoral integration, collaborative policy dialogue, and stakeholder participation.

Community-oriented primary care (COPC) “is a model of primary care which puts into practice the idea that community context plays a role in the health of the person” ([163](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R163), p. 101). Widespread implementation of this vision, espoused for more than six decades ([71](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R71), [83](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R83)), has been limited by the accessibility and linkability of community and clinical data and by fee-for-service payment mechanisms ([98](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R98)). In the Information Age, and time of health care and public health reform, opportunities abound for more fully actualizing the community focus of primary care ([27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R27), [109](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R109), [147](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R147)). An emerging paradigm of primary care/public health integration ([27](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R27)) is evidenced by the recent revival of the concept ([45](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R45)) and examples of “communities of solution” ([56](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R56)) and calls for a “third revolution” ([16](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R16)) of public health focused on quality of life ([40](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R40)).

Health care systems based on primary care have better population health ([82](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R82), [140](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R140), [141](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R141)), less inequality ([25](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R25), [141](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R141)), lower expenditures ([50](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R50), [55](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R55), [82](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R82), [138](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R138)), and greater quality ([4](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R4)). This added value occurs despite apparently poorer quality of care for individual diseases compared with care by specialists in those diseases ([131](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R131)). Thus, the value of primary health care emerges from the complex interaction of its attributes ([127](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R127)).

### Implications for Metrics

The integrative focus of primary care in a fragmented health care system and society has important implications for developing primary health care metrics that do more good than harm. Recent scientific understanding shows that complex systems are more than the sum of their parts ([105](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R105), [144](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R144)). Treating primary health care as a series of commodities, rather than as a set of relationships ([145](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R145)), risks killing the emergent properties of primary care that are responsible for its value. Strong metrics of primary health care assess both the parts and the interactions of those parts ([125](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R125)) to create a whole of health and high-value health care.

The scientific evidence needed to support primary care metrics is often lacking in studies focused on disease-specific care ([125](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R125)) that specifically exclude critical contextual factors ([132](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R132), [150](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R150)) such as the multimorbidity ([47](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R47)) that is common among people seen in primary care ([46](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R46)). Metrics of quality in primary health care must account for context ([132](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R132), [156](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R156)), complexity ([146](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R146)), relationships ([7](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R7)), and emergence ([57](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R57)).

A major asset of primary care is its local adaptability. Recent international comparative research ([92](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R92)) shows that primary care manifests very differently to meet the needs of people in diverse sociopolitical environments ([92](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R92)). Appropriate metrics may vary considerably for these diverse manifestations. It is important to recognize and manage the tension between standardized measurement and the support of desirable heterogeneity based on local needs.

METRICS OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
------------------------------

Below we highlight domains of relevant primary health care metrics and their use to inform performance, purpose, and aspiration.

### Domains of Metrics

The field of primary health care is in the midst of a paradigm shift ([78](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R78)) that is attempting to merge the benefits of personalized care and a population focus. [Table 2](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#T2) shows what we believe are the emerging metrics of primary health care and lists some existing measurement tools from which measures for assessing the emerging metrics might be drawn. As the field evolves, it will be helpful to have support for developing standardized measures. In the United States, the National Committee on Vital Health Statistics would be a logical home for such efforts ([69](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R69)).

#### Table 2.

Metrics of primary health care and some potential sources of measures

| Metrics | Possible sources from existing measures[a](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#TFN1) |
| :--- | :---: |
| Tenets of primary care |  |
| Accessibility | PCAT ([22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R22), [120](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R120)), ACES ([113](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R113)), CAHPS[b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#TFN2), NCQA ([101](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R101)) |
| Whole-person, comprehensive focus | PCAS ([114](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R114)), PCAT ([22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R22), [120](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R120)), CPCI ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R41), [42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R42), [44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R44)) |
| Sustained partnership and personal relationships over time | CQI ([93](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R93)), MHI ([29](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R29)), CPCI ([41](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R41),[42](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R42),[44](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R44)), PCAS ([114](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R114)), PCAT ([22](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R22), [120](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R120)), ACES ([113](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R113)), CARE ([94](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#R94)), CAHPS[b](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC6360939/#TFN2) |

# Most Important Metrics for a Healthcare Quality Management Dashboard

![](https://www.symplr.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/linda-j.jpg)
![](https://www.symplr.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/07/Blog-Image-Template_Nicole2-Untitled-Page-21-scaled.webp)

### When it comes to healthcare, we all want quality over quantity. Value-based care models aim to deliver on this goal by moving us beyond fee-for-service models.

Instead, payers will reimburse providers not for quantity of care but for clinical outcomes tied to cost containment. To succeed in this transition, organizations must implement strategies that effectively track [quality metrics](https://www.symplr.com/glossary/quality-metrics) and improve care while demonstrating evidence-based outcomes. 

## What is a healthcare quality management dashboard?

Healthcare organizations generate vast quantities of quality and other data. Interpreting and managing that data is challenging when stored in disparate systems. [Quality management](https://www.symplr.com/glossary/quality-management) dashboards increase the visibility and transparency of key quality data targets and measures, including:

[Quality dashboards](https://www.symplr.com/glossary/quality-dashboard) display countless metrics and other quality key performance indicators (KPIs) such as:

Nurse leaders and administrators compare this internal performance data against departments, facilities in the system, and other healthcare organizations via benchmarks to identify areas for quality improvement. The visualizations that quality dashboards provide help identify previously hidden patterns in data, deviations from ideal care, and performance differences across units and organizations. Quality dashboards also allow leaders to track responses to operational changes and quality improvement initiatives.  

## What metrics should you include on a healthcare quality management dashboard?

You should choose quality and [risk management](https://www.symplr.com/glossary/risk-management) metrics based on your organization’s needs, the specific reporting requirements of your payers including CMS’ quality programs, and any accrediting bodies such as The Joint Commission.

In 2023, CMS used quality measures in the five measure groups that follow to [rate more than 4,500 hospitals](https://data.cms.gov/provider-data/topics/hospitals/overall-hospital-quality-star-rating/) from one to five stars. Although star ratings for hospitals are not directly related to financial reimbursement, many of the measures used to determine the star ratings are also used in other programs, such as the [Hospital Value-Based Purchasing Program](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/quality/value-based-programs/hospital-purchasing), that directly affect reimbursement. 

### 1. Mortality

The patient mortality rate measures the percentage of patients who die in a hospital’s care before being discharged. Mortality rate is a strong indicator of providers’ ability to stabilize a patient’s condition following surgery or another procedure. 

### 2. Safety

Safety metrics are related to medical incidents, including errors and adverse events. Hospital incidents include unintentional consequences or side effects of hospital procedures, including conditions like sepsis, postoperative respiratory failure, pulmonary embolisms, hemorrhages, and other reactions or infections.

Safety metrics measure healthcare professionals’ ability to provide comprehensive, high-quality patient care without triggering an adverse reaction. The number of harm events per 1,000 patient days is one way to quantify this measure.

Two common patient safety measures are:

![CTA Thumbnail Image](https://www.symplr.com/wp-content/uploads/2024/09/symplr-Website-Pop-Up-Artwork.png)

### Ready to take a Deeper Dive?

Get the full picture on readmission penalties and their financial impact. Discover how predictive analytics can help you avoid millions in penalties.

### 3. Readmission

Readmission metrics refer to the percentage of patients who are admitted to a hospital within 30 days of being discharged for the same condition or a complication from the original episode of care. High hospital readmission rates indicate that physicians and other care providers are not delivering the proper care to patients, perhaps because they are overlooking complications or relevant patient data. Lower hospital readmission rates indicate higher care quality. In 2024, the [average readmission rate](https://www.definitivehc.com/resourceshealthcare-insights/average-hospital-readmission-state) in the US was 14.56%, with an average readmission cost of $16,300 per patient.

Finding the optimal length of stay to prevent readmission is important for patient *and* hospital financial health. High readmission rates can decrease hospital revenue since hospitals with the highest readmission rates may be penalized by not receiving full Medicare reimbursement payments. Hospital readmissions cost Medicare [$26 billion annually](https://www.healthcaredive.com/news/ma-patients-readmission-rates-higher-than-traditional-medicare-study-find/557694/), so it’s not surprising that Medicare is focused on reducing readmission rates. In fiscal year 2023, 74.67% of hospitals in [Medicare’s Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program (HRRP)](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/payment/prospective-payment-systems/acute-inpatient-pps/hospital-readmissions-reduction-program-hrrp) received [readmissions penalties](https://www.advisory.com/daily-briefing/2022/11/04/hrrp-penalties). 

### 4. Patient experience

Patient-reported outcome measures assess patients’ experiences and perceptions of their healthcare. You can evaluate these by [tracking complaints](https://www.symplr.com/products/symplr-safety) and administering [patient satisfaction surveys](https://www.symplr.com/products/symplr-compliance), including the [Hospital Consumer Assessment of Healthcare Providers and Systems (HCAHPS) survey](https://www.cms.gov/medicare/quality/initiatives/hospital-quality-initiative/hcahps-patients-perspectives-care-survey) that measures 64 markers of patient satisfaction ranging from care quality to care facility cleanliness. HCAHPS scores provide important information about how hospitals can improve patient care and services. 

### 5. Timely & effective care

Timeliness of care metrics, such as waiting times from arrival at the ED until diagnostic evaluation, are related to patient access to care. Overcrowding in the ED has been associated with increased inpatient mortality, increased length of stay, and increased costs for admitted patients.

Effectiveness of care outcomes measures evaluate two things: compliance with best practice care guidelines and achieved outcomes. Ensuring clinicians are following current best practice care guidelines is essential to improving clinical outcomes. Monitoring treatment outcomes and notifying clinicians when they need to review care guidelines are also important.

Healthcare quality dashboards are essential tools that provide a visual representation of key performance indicators, helping to monitor and improve [patient care outcomes](https://www.symplr.com/glossary/patient-outcomes). They enable healthcare professionals to identify trends, measure progress against benchmarks, and make data-driven decisions.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/686d4fca96e4813b96758e2b/686d5acdb30a839e5bac7f4e_e82ee4a87c2f9d8bbf0ad562309f1f98_HC%20Logo.svg)

# The Top Seven Healthcare Outcome Measures and Three Measurement Essentials

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/686d4fca96e4813b96758e56/686d4fca96e4813b96759795_Ann-Tinker1-jpeg.webp)

## Summary

Healthcare outcomes improvement canât happen without effective outcomes measurement. Given the healthcare industryâs administrative and regulatory complexities, and the fact that health systems measure and report on hundreds of outcomes annually, this article adds much-needed clarity by reviewing the top seven outcome measures, including definitions, important nuances, and real-life examples. The top seven categories of outcome measures are:

1. Mortality  
2. Readmissions  
3. Safety of care  
4. Effectiveness of care  
5. Patient experience  
6. Timeliness of care  
7. Efficient use of medical imaging

CMS used these seven outcome measures to calculate overall hospital quality and arrive at its 2018 hospital star ratings. This article also reiterates the importance of outcomes measurement, clarifies how outcome measures are defined and prioritized, and recommends three essentials for successful outcomes measurement.

## Downloads

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/plugins/Basic/assets/placeholder.60f9b1840c.svg)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/686d4fca96e4813b96758e56/686d4fca96e4813b96759e0e_6706eb5801ee02681557e973_66f078499f325c0bd53b86ad_healthcare-financial-outcomes-jpeg.webp)
![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/686d4fca96e4813b96758e56/686d4fca96e4813b96759e0e_6706eb5801ee02681557e973_66f078499f325c0bd53b86ad_healthcare-financial-outcomes-jpeg.webp)

The healthcare industry is riddled with administrative and regulatory complexities that make it difficult for health systems to achieve theÂ [Tripleâor better yet, the QuadrupleâAim of healthcare](http://www.ihi.org/communities/blogs/the-triple-aim-or-the-quadruple-aim-four-points-to-help-set-your-strategy). The complexities found in outcomes improvementÂ are particularly challenging, as health systems measure and report on hundreds of these outcomes annually. Health systems can manage these complexities by taking a closer look at outcome measuresâunderstanding their definitions and nuances, reviewing real-world examples, and integrating three essentials for successful outcomes measurement.

## Why Measuring Healthcare Outcomes Is Important

The goal of measuring, reporting, and comparing healthcare outcomes is to achieveÂ [the Quadruple Aim of healthcare](http://www.ihi.org/engage/initiatives/tripleaim/Pages/default.aspx):

The organization behind the Triple Aimâ[the Institute for Healthcare Improvement (IHI)](http://www.ihi.org/Pages/default.aspx)âis dedicated to outcomes improvement. IHI describes measurement as âa critical part of testing and implementing changes. Measures tell a team whether the changes they are making actually lead to improvement.â The fourth aim may vary depending on the organization.

Healthcare organizationsâmotivated by the Quadruple Aimâmeasure outcomes for several reasons:

## Outcome Measures Defined

TheÂ [World Health Organization](https://www.who.int/en/)Â defines an outcome measure as a âchange in the health of an individual, group of people, or population that is attributable to an intervention or series of interventions.â Outcome measures (mortality, readmission, patient experience, etc.) are the quality and cost targets healthcare organizations are trying to improve.

Outcome measures are frequently reported to the government, commercial payers, and organizations that report on quality, such asÂ [The LeapFrog Group](https://www.leapfroggroup.org/)âa national nonprofit that evaluates and reports U.S. hospital safety and quality performance. LeapFrogâs work centers on âincreasing transparency among health care providers in order to reduce the estimatedÂ [440,000 annual deaths from hospital errors, accidents, and injuries](https://www.hospitalsafetygrade.org/what-is-patient-safety/errors-injuries-accidents-infections).â While initial measures focused on inpatient care, they have since expanded to include most aspects of care delivery.

## Outcome Measures Are Driven by National Standards and Financial Incentives

Outcome measures are primarily defined and prioritized by national organizations, includingÂ [CMS](https://www.cms.gov/),Â [The Joint Commission](https://www.jointcommission.org/), and theÂ [National Association for Healthcare Quality (NAHQ)](https://nahq.org/). Health systems target outcome measures based on state and federal government mandates, accreditation requirements, and financial incentives.

Although healthcare outcomes and targets are defined at the national level, health systems might set more aggressive targets. Meeting and exceeding these national targets, benefits not only quality of care, but also healthcare organizationsâ marketing and contracting efforts.

Reporting and accreditation entities have processes in place to normalize outcomes data to account for context, which is key when it comes to reporting. Itâs easy to take data out of context. Using fall rates as an example, if a small, 10-bed hospital sees 10 patients in one month and one patient falls, then their fall rate is high (10 percent).

The Joint Commission is a regulatory body that accredits health systems and has national standards for quality measures that are âdeveloped with input from healthcare professionals, providers, subject matter experts, consumers, government agencies (including CMS) and employers.â New standards must meet the following strict requirements:

CMS uses outcome measures to calculate overall hospital quality. In a 2018 report,Â [CMS explained how it arrived at its 2018 hospital star ratings](https://www.medicare.gov/hospitalcompare/Data/Measure-groups.html). CMS grouped outcome measures into seven categories weighted by importance:

## The Top Seven Healthcare Outcome Measures Explained

There are hundreds of outcome measures, ranging from changes in blood pressure in patients with hypertension to [patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs)](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/insights/top-7-healthcare-outcome-measures). The seven groupings of outcome measures CMS uses to calculate hospital quality are some of the most common in healthcare:

### #1: Mortality

Mortality is an essential population healthÂ outcome measure. For example,Â [Piedmont Healthcareâs evidence-based care standardization](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/success-stories/pneumonia-mortality-piedmont)Â for pneumonia patients, resulted in a 56.5 percent relative reduction in the pneumonia mortality rate.

### #2: Safety of Care

Safety of care outcome measures pertain to medical mistakes. Skin breakdown and hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) are common safety of care outcome measures:

### #3: Readmissions

Readmission following hospitalization is a common outcome measure. Readmission is costly (and often preventable). In fact, researchers estimate that in one year, [$25 to $45 billion is spent on avoidable complications and unnecessary hospital readmissions](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/success-stories/reduce-hospital-readmission-rates-utmb). After increasing efforts to reduce their hospital readmission rate, [the University of Texas Medical Branch (UTMB) saw a 14.5 percent relative reduction in their 30-day all-cause readmission rate](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/success-stories/reduce-hospital-readmission-rates-utmb), resulting in $1.9 million in cost avoidance. UTMB reduced their hospital readmission rate by implementing several care coordination programs and leveraging their analytics platform and advanced analytics applications to improve the accuracy and timeliness of data for informing decision making and monitoring performance.

### #4: Patient Experience

Patient-reported outcome measures (PROMs) fall within the patient experience outcome measure category. According to theÂ [Agency for Clinical Innovation](https://www.aci.health.nsw.gov.au/make-it-happen/prms/about-patient-reported-measures)Â (ACI), PROMs âassess the patientâs experience and perception of their healthcare. This information can provide a more realistic gauge of patient satisfaction as well as real-time information for local service improvement and to enable a more rapid response to identified issues.â For example, a patient might be asked to complete a satisfaction survey (on a scale of 1-5) about the care they received.

Patient experience may also be used as aÂ [balance metric for improvement work](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/insights/process-vs-outcome-measures-healthcare). For example, a care delivery process may decrease the LOS, which can be a positive outcome, but result in a decreased patient satisfaction score if patients instead feel they are being pushed out.

### #5: Effectiveness of Care

Effectiveness of care outcome measures evaluate two things:

Given the rapid changes that occur within healthcare, making sure best practice care guidelines are current is critical for achieving the best care outcomes. Itâs important to track clinician compliance with care guidelines; Itâs equally important to monitor treatment outcomes and alert clinicians when care guidelines need to be reviewed.

Failing to adhere to evidence-based care guidelines can have negative consequences for patients. For example, according toÂ [The Dartmouth Atlas of Healthcare](https://www.dartmouthatlas.org/), âeven though it is well established that beta-blockers can reduce the risk of heart attack in patients who have already had one heart attack, many heart attack patients are never prescribed beta-blockers.â

### #6: Timeliness of Care

Timeliness of care outcome measures assess patient access to care. Overcrowding in the emergency department has been associated with increased inpatient mortality, increased length of stay, and increased costs for admitted patients.

A community hospital systemÂ [implemented an improvement process to address overcrowding in its ED](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/success-stories/emergency-department-triage-mission-health)Â after determining that approximately 4,000 patients were leaving its ED each year without being seen. They leveraged their analytics platform to develop an ED analytics application that provided actionable, timely ED performance data to focus improvement efforts on four areas: staffing patterns, registration, triage assessment by the registered nurse, and early access to a qualified medical provider. They achieved significant performance improvements, including an 89 percent relative reduction in the rate of patients that left without being seen, with current performance at 0.4 percent.

### #7: Efficient Use of Medical Imaging

The efficient use of medical imaging is an increasingly important outcome measure. According to theÂ [European Science Foundation](http://www.esf.org/), âMedical imaging plays a central role in the global healthcare system as it contributes to improved patient outcome and more cost-efficient healthcare in all major disease entities.â

For example, duringÂ [Texas Childrenâs Hospitalâs efforts to improve asthma care](https://www.healthcatalyst.com/learn/success-stories/launching-an-enterprise-data-warehouse-to-rapidly-reduce-waste-in-asthma-care)Â it discovered a high volume of chest X-rays being administered to asthma patients. Using its EDW to examine real-time X-ray data, it realized clinicians were ordering chest X-rays for 65 percent of their asthma patientsâevidence-based practice calls for X-rays in only five percent of cases. Texas Childrenâs Hospitalâs IT team traced the problem to a faulty order set within the hospitalâs EHR, and rewrote the order set to reflect the evidence-based best practice.

## Process Measures Are Equally Important

Achieving outcomes is important, but the process by which health systems achieve outcomes is equally important. Process measures capture provider productivity and adherence to standards of recommended care. For example, if a health system wants to reduce the incidence of skin breakdown, then it might implement the process measure of performing a risk assessment using theÂ [Barden Scale](https://www.in.gov/isdh/files/Braden_Scale.pdf)Â for reducing pressure ulcer risk in all the appropriate units in the hospital. If health systems are too focused on an outcome, then they lose sight of the process.

The following outcome and process measures illustrate how systems can improve healthcare outcomes by improving processes:

## Three Essentials for Successful Healthcare Outcomes Measurement

Among every health systemâs goals is to improve patient outcomes. But outcomes improvement canât happen without effective outcomes measurement. As health systems work diligently to achieve the Quadruple Aim, they need to prioritize three outcomes measurement essentials: transparency, integrated care, and interoperability.

Used in tandem, these essentials improve and sustain outcomes measurement efforts by creating a data-driven culture that embraces data transparency, an integrated care environment that treats the whole patient and improves critical care transitions, and interoperable systems that enable the seamless exchange of outcomes measurement data between clinicians, departments, and hospitals.

### #1: Data Transparency

Healthcare is on a journey to outcomes transparency. Patients rely on outcomes data to make educated decisions about their healthcare. Quality reporting organizations, such asÂ [The LeapFrog Group](https://www.leapfroggroup.org/), evaluate and report on U.S. hospital safety and quality performance. Patients want reassurance that theyâre receiving the best care for the lowest cost. Publicly reported healthcare outcomes help do just that.

### #2: Integrated Care and Transitions of Care

The industry is also shifting toward integrated careâhospitals arenât just treating a hip anymore; theyâre treating the whole person. A key component of integrated care is helping patients with transitions: easing patient transitions from the ER, to surgery, to inpatient care, to rehab, and, ultimately, back to a steady, normal state. Transitional points of care are critical for managing consistency of care and providing the right care in the right setting at the lowest cost.

### #3: Data Interoperability

Sharing data between departments within an integrated system is another important component. Outcomes measurement and improvement depends on the systemâs ability to share data across clinicians, labs, hospitals, clinics, pharmacies, and other staff, departments, and settings. EDWs improve interoperability by integrating data and providing a single source of truth.

Improving critical care transitions through integrated care and seamlessly exchanging data through interoperability are essential ingredients for better outcomes measurement. For example, as heart failure patients are discharged (depending on the risk stratification), itâs critical for them to see a cardiologist or primary care physician as quickly as possible. Otherwise, they have a higher risk of being readmitted.

## The Quadruple Aim: The Goal of Outcomes Measurement

Outcomes measurement should always tie back to the Quadruple Aim, so healthcare organizations arenât just reporting numbers. Health systems shouldnât become so obsessed with numbers that they forget their Quadruple Aim goal. Instead, they should focus on quality and improving the care experience at the most efficient cost.

Health systems measure outcomes to ensure they are delivering the best care for patients and providing a transparent, efficient, and accessible environment for all healthcare providers. That is outcomes nirvana.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Health outcomes improvement is measured through metrics like mortality rates and readmissions, indicating quality of care and effectiveness of treatments, ultimately enhancing patient health and satisfaction.
Resource allocation efficiency evaluates how well healthcare resources are utilized, focusing on minimizing waste and ensuring optimal distribution to meet community health needs effectively.

Public health response effectiveness gauges the capacity of health systems to address crises, ensuring timely interventions and resource mobilization, thus enhancing community resilience against health threats.
